ISEN 302 Syllabus

Section 502 (11194)

Economic Analysis of Engineering Projects

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: MW

Start Time: 12:40PM

End Time: 1:30PM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ZACH 344

Credit Hours:  2

Instructor Details

Annette Rodgers

Email:  amrodgers@tamu.edu

Office Location:  ETB 4040

Phone:  979-845-2358

Office Hours

M & W by appointment/ Tue 10:00 am – 1:00 -   Zoom by appointment only

Preferred Contact Method

email

Biography

Mrs. Rodgers is an experienced professional with 38 years in space operations,

hardware design, and multidisciplinary engineering. I’ve trained astronauts and

engineers for Space Shuttle and ISS operations, and served as a flight controller

for payload operations. I spent 18 years as a liaison for the Japan Aerospace

Exploration Agency (JAXA) to the ISS, managing scientific payloads and

leading the installation of the Japanese Segment. As Vice President at JAMSS

America, I oversaw operations, technical programs, and ISO 9001 certification.

I’ve also managed NASA contracts for eVTOL technology development.

Currently, I teach Engineering Economic Analysis as an Associate Professor of

Practice at Texas A&M University.

Socials

N/A

Catalog Description

Economic Analysis of Engineering Projects. (2-0). Credit 2. Principles of economic

equivalence; time value of money; analysis of single and multiple investments;

comparison of alternatives; capital recovery and after-tax analysis of economic

projects. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in MATH 152 or MATH 172; also taught

at Qatar campus.

Course Prerequisites

MATH 152 or MATH 172 and ability to use Microsoft Excel.

Required math pre-requisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in MATH 152 or MATH 172; also

taught at Qatar campus.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Make engineering decisions based on an economic analysis of alternatives

Explain and demonstrate the principles of economic equivalence and time value of

money

Formulate and solve equations using interest factors and formulas

Distinguish between nominal and effective interest rates and convert between them

Identify and determine constituent cash flows of single and/or multiple investments

Make engineering decisions based on an economic analysis of alternatives

Explain and demonstrate the principles of economic equivalence and time value of

money

Formulate and solve equations using interest factors and formulas

Distinguish between nominal and effective interest rates and convert between them

Identify and determine constituent cash flows of single and/or multiple investments

Examine, diagram, and compare investment alternatives on an economic basis using

appropriate terminology

Conclude and justify the best investment alternative(s) based on appropriate analysis

Develop and construct spreadsheet models to investigate alternatives

Determine and utilize appropriate capital recovery methods for alternatives

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Engineering Economic Analysis (14 th Edition)

ISBN:  9780190063467 e-version

Authors:  D. Newnan, T. G. Eschenbach, J. P. Lavelle, and N. Lewis (February 19,

2019)

Publisher:  Oxford University Press

Publication Date:  (February 19, 2019)

Edition:  14th Edition

URL for Resource:  https://www.vitalsource.com/products/engineering-economic-

analysis-don-newnan-ted-eschenbach-v9780190931940?term=9780190063467

Notes:

We have been notified that this textbook is no longer available in print version. However, the

edition is available with a new (ISBN: 9780190063467) as it is now included as a pack and

contains an access code. The option I recommend is to buy the digital version of the 14 th

edition, at the bookstore or anywhere you can find it at a lesser price. I am not aware of what

the bookstore charges for the book, but I am adding a link below where you may purchase it

for $49.99 if that price is better:

https://www.vitalsource.com/products/engineering-economic-analysis-don-newnan-ted-

eschenbach-v9780190931940?term=9780190063467

Grading Policy

Homework assignments are due on the due date posted on Canvas. Please submit all work

through Canvas. You should submit your work on time.  Late submissions are penalized

10% per each late day and are only accepted on the week the work is due, after that, no

late work will be accepted.  Please note that homework assignments have different value and

weight, so please do not assume they are all equal. In addition, there might be in-class

assignments / checking for understanding, short quizzes, in-class quizzes, three tests during

the semester, and one special assignment worth a test grade. There is NO FINAL for my

class. You will be given more information after the semester begins. No make-up

examinations will be allowed unless prior arrangements have been made, or if you have a

assignments / checking for understanding, short quizzes, in-class quizzes, three tests during

the semester, and one special assignment worth a test grade. There is NO FINAL for my

class. You will be given more information after the semester begins. No make-up

examinations will be allowed unless prior arrangements have been made, or if you have a

university accepted excuse. Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused

absence is not considered “late” and is exempted from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ) ,

but this HAS to be pre-arranged with the professor .

The components of the course grade and their weights are as follows: Assignments, 20%;

Quizzes, 20%; Tests, 60%. The university letter grade policy is followed (>=90%: A; 80-

89%: B; 70 – 79%: C; 60 – 69%: D; below 60%: F.)

There will be no curve at the end of this semester, so please make sure you submit your work

on time!

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

The course website will be maintained through  http://canvas.tamu.edu/ .  This site will contain

announcements and other information concerning the course. In addition, it will be used to

distribute class materials (homework assignment, notes,) and grades. Check the website

regularly, I will send last minute information and communication through Canvas in the

“ Announcements ” section of Canvas. Any information posted there will be as valid as if it was

mentioned in class.

It is expected that all work submitted in this class will be done individually. If you got any

help with homework, please cite that person in your write-up. For example, “I thank John

Smith for helping me solve this problem.” If you worked together with one or more other

people, then each of you should cite the other(s).

A major reason for taking this class is to prepare all engineering majors for the Fundamentals

of Engineering (FE) exam, as well as to prepare Industrial Engineering majors for other ISEN

courses. To this end, students are strongly encouraged to use calculators that are approved by

NCEES for use on the FE. The NCEES website ( http://ncees.org/exams/calculator-policy/ )

provides a list of the current approved calculator brands and models.

All exams are administered through Canvas using Respondus proctoring software. They

will be closed-book/notes/smartphone. Calculator is allowed if needed. If you have

requested special accommodations, you DO NOT need to schedule your test through the

provides a list of the current approved calculator brands and models.

All exams are administered through Canvas using Respondus proctoring software. They

will be closed-book/notes/smartphone. Calculator is allowed if needed. If you have

requested special accommodations, you DO NOT need to schedule your test through the

testing center. I will add any extra time as required. Please come speak to me if you have

questions.

Course Schedule

Time durations for the topics are tentative and depend on class responses.

Week 1 (01/12) Introduction / Ch. 1 Making Economic Decisions

Week 2 (01/19)  MLK   Holiday (01/19- no school)

(01/14) Ch. 2 Engineering Costs and Estimating

Week 3 (01/26) Ch. 2 Engineering Costs and Estimating

Week 4 (02/02) Ch. 3 Interest and Equivalence

Week 5 (02/09) Ch. 4 Equivalence for Repeated Cash Flows

Week 6 (02/16) Ch. 4 /Review  Exam. 1: Test Chapters 1 - 4 (02/18)

Week 7 (02/23) Ch. 5 Present Worth Analysis

Week 8 (03/02) Ch. 6 Annual Cash Flow Analysis

Week 9 (03/09)  Spring Break - No school

Week 10 (03/16) Ch. 7 Rate of Return Analysis / ABET Assignment (Due 03/22)

Week 11 (03/23) E xam. 2: Test Chapters 5 - 7 (03/23)  / Ch. 8 Choosing the Best Alternative

Week 12 (03/30) Ch. 9 Other Analysis Techniques / Chapter 11 Depreciation

Week 13 (04/06) Ch. 11 Depreciation

Week 14 (04/13) CH 12 Income taxes for Corporations

Week 15 (04/20)  Exam. 3: Test Chapters 8 - 12 (04/20)

Week 16 (04/27)    Special Topic / Last day of Class 4/27 (No testing allowed)

Week 17 (05/04) Finals Week

Additional Course Information

Outside of my office hours, you MUST set up an appointment.  If you would like to set up

a meeting, please email me 24 hours before the meeting time and provide a few times that

might work with your schedule. I will try to find a time that works for both. You should

prepare questions before we meet.

AI Statement

Intellectual honesty is vital to an academic community and for my fair evaluation of

your work. All work submitted in this course must be your own, completed in

accordance with the University’s academic regulations. You may not engage in

unauthorized collaboration or make use of ChatGPT or other AI composition

software.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance is mandatory.  Students enrolled in the online section are expected to

complete the weekly work at the same pace as the live sections, unless they have a

university approved excuse.
